planting.note: Direct sow in fall into shaded borders, wall crevices, or rock gardens; yellow corydalis self-seeds freely once established and cold stratification from winter temperatures triggers reliable spring germination.

directions: Direct sow in fall in partial to full shade, allowing natural cold stratification over winter to break dormancy; yellow corydalis thrives in the thin, gritty soil of wall crevices, rocky outcrops, and shaded borders where it naturalizes freely by self-seeding. Pseudofumaria lutea produces ferny, blue-green foliage and an exceptionally long succession of small, spurred yellow tubular flowers from spring through fall, making it one of the longest-blooming shade perennials available. Once established it requires little care and will colonize appropriate niches throughout the garden, though it is shallow-rooted and never aggressively weedy.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich soil; Asian wood poppy self-seeds readily and winter cold provides the stratification needed for reliable spring germination.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade, or allow established plants to self-seed into adjacent areas; Asian wood poppy performs best in conditions similar to its native Chinese mountain woodland habitat with cool, moist summers. Stylophorum lasiocarpum is the Chinese counterpart to the North American celandine poppy (S. diphyllum), producing deeply lobed, pinnate foliage and large, brilliant golden-yellow four-petaled flowers over a long spring-to-summer season. It self-seeds freely but without becoming weedy, naturalizing into attractive informal colonies beneath deciduous trees where its foliage remains ornamental well past bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; eomecon spreads vigorously by stolons and should be sited where it can colonize freely or be contained by hard edging.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade and allow stolons to spread freely; eomecon is most effective when given space to form a weed-suppressing groundcover colony beneath large shrubs or trees. Eomecon chionantha is a distinctive Chinese woodland perennial producing large, scalloped, glaucous blue-green leaves on long petioles and bearing delicate white four-petaled flowers resembling small single poppies from late spring through early summer. When stems or roots are cut, the plant exudes a striking orange-red latex sap; it spreads readily by underground stolons and can colonize significant areas, making it both a vigorous groundcover and a plant that requires thoughtful siting.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to wet soil in full to partial sun; creeping eryngo tolerates temporary flooding and is well suited to rain gardens, pond margins, and low, wet areas of the native landscape.

directions: Direct sow in spring in moist to occasionally wet soil in full to partial sun; creeping eryngo is highly adaptable to temporarily flooded sites and performs well in rain gardens and low-lying areas where other perennials fail. Eryngium prostratum is a native eryngo of the southeastern United States, spreading prostrate stems to form low mats studded with small, pale blue to white thistle-like flower heads from summer through fall that attract native bees and beneficial insects. Unlike the showy ornamental eryngos, this species is valued for its ecological function as a native groundcover tolerant of flooding, drought recovery, and partial shade at the woodland edge.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow winter cold stratification to break seed dormancy; purple milkweed benefits from natural cold-moist treatment over winter and will germinate reliably the following spring.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, moderately fertile soil in full to partial sun, allowing winter cold stratification to trigger spring germination; purple milkweed is slower to establish than common milkweed but grows from a deep taproot and should not be moved once established. Asclepias purpurascens is a native milkweed of central and eastern North America, producing upright stems with large opposite leaves and rounded umbels of intensely deep rose-purple flowers in June and July that are highly attractive to monarch butterflies, which use it as a host plant. It is less commonly grown than A. tuberosa or A. incarnata but is particularly valuable for partial-shade woodland edge gardens where it thrives and provides essential monarch breeding habitat.
